title: Hyderabad weather: IMD predicts rain and drop in temperatures after heatwave - News9live
full_text:
Hyderabad weather: IMD predicts rain and drop in temperatures after heatwave
Hyderabad is set for a major weather shift in the first week of May as IMD predicts a significant temperature drop below 40°C and above-normal rainfall, providing much-needed relief after a record-breaking heatwave in April.
New Delhi: After a brutal April where the mercury pushed past 45°C in several districts, the city is finally set to receive some breathing room. On Friday, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) in Hyderabad confirmed that the first week of May is set to bring a welcome shift, marked by a dip in temperatures and above-normal rainfall across Telangana. While the state has been sizzling, the transition to cooler weather will be a gradual process varying from one region to another.
Hyderabad prediction for today
The temperature in the surrounding districts like Medchal-Malkajgiri, Rangareddy, or Sangareddy will witness extreme heat. While the city is expected to see maximum temperatures stabilise comfortably under the 40°C mark.
As per the weather forecast, the temperature is a bit high at 40°C with sunny conditions during the day, transitioning to partly cloudy in the night with a low of 27°C.
While the sun will still be out, the oppressive heatwave feel is fading. The residents in Vikarabad and Bhongir can also expect this immediate respite as the peak summer intensity begins to wane.
What to expect in the coming week?
The outlook for the rest of Telangana for the first week of May is looking much wetter and cooler than usual. A cyclonic circulation over interior Maharashtra, combined with a trough stretching down to Tamil Nadu, is expected to trigger rainfall across the region. While Hyderabad gets early relief the northern districts like Adilabad and Nizamabad will have to tough it out for about three more days with temperatures still hovering between 41°C and 44°C.
However, by May 4, the rain is expected to hit these northern pockets too which will bring the mercury down. Overall, the IMD predicts that daytime temperatures throughout the first week of May will remain below the normal average for this time of year, offering a rare and much-needed break from the gruelling summer sun.
